prompt: Research on the price dynamics of chub mackerel in major aquatic markets of Pacific Rim countries, and its interannual variations in weight/length. Combined with oceanographic theory, these research findings can further establish direct correlations between high-quality marine biological resources, aquatic markets, fishery economics, and the marine environment.
article:
### 1) Scope, definitions, and key caveat (species / product ambiguity)

**Chub mackerel** here refers to *Scomber japonicus*. In Pacific Rim trade and statistics, however, “mackerel” frequently **mixes species** (e.g., *S. japonicus* with **blue mackerel** *S. australasicus* in Japanese landing statistics, and **Atlantic mackerel** *S. scombrus* as a major import into East Asia). This matters because price series can be **quality- and species-segmented**, weakening cross-market comparability and price transmission.

Two concrete examples of this ambiguity:

* **Japan**: some prefectures report chub + blue as aggregated “mackerels,” and chub shares are estimated by monthly subsampling and applied to total “mackerels” landings [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf).  
* **Korea**: domestic supply is strongly influenced by imported Norwegian (Atlantic) mackerel, which can dominate distribution and affect observed “mackerel” prices even when domestic chub mackerel conditions differ [[16]](https://korea.stripes.com/food-drink/mackerel-prices-busan.html), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482).

Throughout, I treat “major aquatic markets” as the main **price formation points** (landing/auction/wholesale hubs) and **official/semiofficial price reporting systems** used by industry and policy, and I explicitly flag where a dataset is “mackerel (mixed)” vs chub-specific.

---

## 2) Major aquatic markets and price reporting systems around the Pacific Rim (since ~2000)

### 2.1 Japan — central wholesale markets (Toyosu/Tokyo) + purse-seine landing system

**Market structure**
* Japan’s central wholesale markets are classic **auction/wholesale price formation nodes**. Tokyo’s main hub is **Toyosu Market** (successor to Tsukiji).

**Price reporting datasets**
* The Tokyo Metropolitan Government publishes daily and monthly central wholesale market statistics; these are republished with clear provenance by GD Freak for **Toyosu mackerel**:
  * “Mackerel (domestic)” monthly average wholesale price (JPY/kg) and volumes based on Tokyo Central Wholesale Market daily report and monthly statistics [[1]](https://jp.gdfreak.com/public/detail/jp011013999600100250/3).  
  * “Frozen mackerel” monthly average wholesale price (JPY/kg), similarly sourced [[2]](https://jp.gdfreak.com/public/detail/jp011013999600100640/3).

**Illustrative recent interannual change (Toyosu)**
* GD Freak reports (examples):
  * **Domestic mackerel** January 2026: **811 JPY/kg**, ~33% higher than January prior year [[1]](https://jp.gdfreak.com/public/detail/jp011013999600100250/3).  
  * **Frozen mackerel** January 2026: **1,121 JPY/kg**, ~38% higher than January prior year [[2]](https://jp.gdfreak.com/public/detail/jp011013999600100640/3).  
These kinds of jumps are consistent with tight supply and/or quality shifts and are useful for diagnosing interannual volatility, but species composition (chub vs blue vs “mackerel” category) must be checked market-by-market.

**Supply-management and price regime shifts (Japan)**
* In Japan’s EEZ purse-seine fishery targeting chub and blue mackerel, an **industry-initiated vessel-level monthly catch limit system introduced in 2007** is associated with a statistically significant **price increase of ~21.3 yen/kg** in treated ports (difference-in-differences), and it “virtually eliminated price gaps” with other regions [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308597X22001002). Mechanistically, this is consistent with **reducing “race-to-fish” gluts**, smoothing landings over time, and improving average realized prices.

---

### 2.2 South Korea — Busan Cooperative Fish Market + national wholesale/retail price monitoring (aT/KAMIS)

**Market structure**
* A key primary node is the **Busan Cooperative Fish Market**, where mackerel is auctioned; a documented example shows an auction with ~500 tons traded on Jan. 4, 2021 [[15]](https://en.yna.co.kr/view/PYH20210104010400315).  
* Wholesale price surges are reported directly from Busan Cooperative Fish Market metrics (e.g., January auction volumes and average auction price) [[16]](https://korea.stripes.com/food-drink/mackerel-prices-busan.html), [[56]](https://www.hapskorea.com/mackerel-prices-surge-at-busan-fish-market-as-exports-and-supply-pressures-mount/).

**Price reporting datasets**
* Korea Agro-Fisheries & Food Trade Corporation **aT** provides distribution price information; Korean reporting cites **KAMIS** (aT’s distribution information system) for retail prices of mackerel by product/grade [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/).  
* Korean market analysis and media regularly combine:
  * Busan auction prices/volumes [[16]](https://korea.stripes.com/food-drink/mackerel-prices-busan.html), [[56]](https://www.hapskorea.com/mackerel-prices-surge-at-busan-fish-market-as-exports-and-supply-pressures-mount/)
  * KAMIS retail price series for graded products [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/)
  * KMI (Korea Maritime Institute) catch and size-composition indicators [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482)

**Size/grade conventions that directly map biology to price**
* Korean reporting repeatedly highlights consumer preference for **medium-to-large fish (often “>300 g”)**, and price rises have been explained by a collapse in the share of medium/large fish even when total catches rise [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482). This is an unusually explicit, policy-relevant bridge between **interannual size structure** and **market price dynamics**.

---

### 2.3 China — wholesale market price reports (GLOBEFISH/CAPPMA) + export-oriented frozen grading

**Market structure & product forms**
* Much of the visible traded form in Chinese export channels is **frozen whole round**, plus processed forms (HGT/HG, fillets, flaps), sold in **weight bands** (e.g., 200–300 g) and **count grades** (IQF counts) [[6]](https://www.shuangying-aquatic.com/), [[7]](https://www.caharborfood.com/pacific-mackerel/frozen-mackerel-scomber-japonicus.html), [[8]](https://zshaohai.goldsupplier.com/665791-Mackerel-Fish/).

**Price reporting datasets**
* FAO/GLOBEFISH + **CAPPMA** publish a recurring **Chinese Fish Price Report** with “Aquatic Products Monthly Price Report in Main Wholesale Markets in China,” updated through at least March 2024 [[9]](https://admin.fishnet.ru/upload2/File/%D0%BE%D1%82%D1%87%D1%91%D1%82(1).pdf). This is one of the most credible cross-market sources for China wholesale prices because it is positioned as an industry–institution product under Ministry supervision [[9]](https://admin.fishnet.ru/upload2/File/%D0%BE%D1%82%D1%87%D1%91%D1%82(1).pdf).  
(Practical limitation: the excerpts available here show the format and grading approach but do not confirm that chub mackerel is included in the displayed pages [[10]](https://openknowledge.fao.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/45f6f039-22fa-4b91-a00b-ba208ae5ad30/content).)

**Trade-code infrastructure**
* Internationally, frozen mackerel is commonly tracked under HS codes that aggregate multiple *Scomber* species. One documented definition is **HTS 0303.54**: “Mackerel (*Scomber scombrus, S. australasicus, S. japonicus*), frozen” [[42]](https://www.datamyne.com/hts/03/030354). This is important for using customs **unit values** as a price proxy, but it inherently mixes species.

---

### 2.4 Taiwan — official wholesale series for “mackerels”

* CEIC republishes an official monthly wholesale price series from Taiwan’s Council of Agriculture: **“Wholesale Price: Fishery Product: Mackerels”** in NTD/kg, from **Jan 2003 to at least Jul 2018** [[26]](https://www.ceicdata.com/en/taiwan/wholesale-price-fishery-product/wholesale-price-fishery-product-mackerels).  
* The series shows very large variability (e.g., all-time high 57.5 NTD/kg in Jun 2018; record low 9.9 NTD/kg in Apr 2003) [[26]](https://www.ceicdata.com/en/taiwan/wholesale-price-fishery-product/wholesale-price-fishery-product-mackerels).  
This is a strong backbone for interannual analysis in Taiwan, but it is a “mackerels” aggregate rather than guaranteed chub-only.

---

### 2.5 United States (West Coast) — ex-vessel (dockside) value/landings reporting + PFMC assessment ecosystem

**Market structure**
* The U.S. system is primarily **landing-ticket / dealer reporting** that produces **ex-vessel value** at the dock rather than a single centralized fish auction.

**Price reporting datasets**
* NOAA Fisheries **Commercial Fisheries Landings** provides queryable landings and **ex-vessel dollar value** by species/state/month from **1990 onward** (and longer for annual summaries) [[29]](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/sustainable-fisheries/commercial-fisheries-landings).  
* PacFIN provides fish-ticket based landings and implied price-per-pound; report definitions include ex-vessel revenue and “average revenue per landed weight pound” [[30]](https://pacfin.psmfc.org/pacfin_pub/data_rpts_pub/all_sp_rpts_pub/r307_woc14.txt).  
* Species-specific national reporting exists: in “Fisheries of the United States 2011,” **chub mackerel** landings were 3 million lbs valued $330k; California accounted for 100% and average ex-vessel price was **$0.11/lb** [[31]](https://www.fws.gov/doiddata/dwh-ar-documents/1187/DWH-AR0007911.pdf).  

**Assessment/survey linkage**
* U.S. PFMC management since 2000 relies on periodic benchmark assessments and catch-only updates [[73]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2025/05/catch-only-stock-assessment-of-pacific-mackerel-scomber-japonicus-for-u-s-management-in-the-2025-26-and-2026-27-fishing-years.pdf/), with a strong dependence on the SWFSC **acoustic-trawl method** surveys for CPS abundance indices [[84]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2019/06/appendix-b-pacific-mackerel-stock-assessment-june-2019.pdf), [[85]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2023/05/g-2-attachment-2-star-panel-report.pdf/).

---

### 2.6 Canada — landed value by province (mackerel category)

* DFO publishes commercial landed value by province; “mackerel” appears as a line item (category-level) with a BC value reported (e.g., 2023 BC mackerel landed value 61 thousand dollars) [[34]](https://www.dfo-mpo.gc.ca/stats/commercial/land-debarq/sea-maritimes/s2023pv-eng.htm).  
This is more useful for macro value trends than chub-specific market dynamics.

---

### 2.7 Mexico — market price infrastructure exists (SNIIM, La Nueva Viga) + national fisheries statistics (CONAPESCA)

* Mexico’s Secretariat of Economy runs **SNIIM** with a “Mercados Pesqueros” section and a reference to the **Mercado de la Nueva Viga** (a major fish market hub) [[108]](http://www.economia-sniim.gob.mx/SNIIM-PESCA/e_lvini1.asp), [[109]](http://www.economia-sniim.gob.mx/nuevo/Home.aspx).  
* CONAPESCA provides a long-running **Anuario Estadístico de Acuacultura y Pesca** repository with coverage from at least **2000 onward** (including downloadable “bases de datos”) [[110]](https://www.gob.mx/conapesca/documentos/anuario-estadistico-de-acuacultura-y-pesca).  
However, in the evidence retrieved here we do not yet have extracted Mexico chub-mackerel price series values—only confirmation that the institutional data infrastructure exists [[108]](http://www.economia-sniim.gob.mx/SNIIM-PESCA/e_lvini1.asp)–[[110]](https://www.gob.mx/conapesca/documentos/anuario-estadistico-de-acuacultura-y-pesca).

---

### 2.8 Peru — caballa (*S. japonicus*) in a CHD-oriented system + market bulletins

Peru is unusually clear (in the sources retrieved) in connecting caballa to direct human consumption channels.

**Product forms and channel split**
* PRODUCE OEE “Ficha técnica” states caballa is destined **exclusively to direct human consumption** (CHD) [[99]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/5.-Ficha-Recurso-Caballa_Ano-2025_revJV.pdf).  
* The same document gives a 2025 destination split: **Fresco 44.7%, Congelado 33.0%, Enlatado 22.3%** [[99]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/5.-Ficha-Recurso-Caballa_Ano-2025_revJV.pdf).  
* Peru’s 2023 fisheries yearbook confirms caballa landings are allocated to CHD forms—fresh, canned, frozen, cured—with no allocation to fishmeal/oil (“Harina” marked “-”) [[100]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Anuario_Estadistico-de-Pesca-y-Acuicultura_2023_09.09.2023-1-1.pdf).

**Price reporting**
* The caballa fiche reports caballa is sold in wholesale fish markets at an **average value of 7.5 soles/kg** [[99]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/5.-Ficha-Recurso-Caballa_Ano-2025_revJV.pdf). (The fiche does not specify the exact averaging window in the excerpt.)  
* PRODUCE maintains a portal page for **Mercado Mayorista Pesquero** with downloadable “Abastecimiento Mercado Pesquero” bulletins and a specific resource: “Precio del Bonito, Jurel y Caballa en mercados pesqueros” [[101]](https://ogeiee.produce.gob.pe/index.php/informacion-sectorial/pesca/mercado-mayorista-pesquero). This indicates a recurring bulletin-based price reporting system (but values were not extracted in the provided material).

**Management as a supply driver**
* Peru’s caballa fiche lists explicit quotas by fleet segment for 2025 (global 79,013 t, split between larger-scale and artisanal) [[99]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/5.-Ficha-Recurso-Caballa_Ano-2025_revJV.pdf), and shows large interannual landing changes (e.g., 40k t in 2025 vs 61k t in 2024, -34.7%) [[99]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/5.-Ficha-Recurso-Caballa_Ano-2025_revJV.pdf)—a direct structural basis for interannual price variability.

---

### 2.9 Chile — terminal wholesale price system (ODEPA) + export unit values + landing certification

**Wholesale price reporting**
* ODEPA maintains “series históricas” of fish/seafood prices at the terminal fish market and provides detailed methodology: daily max/min/common (“common” = modal price), and weekly/monthly “common” is a **volume-weighted average of daily common prices**, nominal and VAT-excluded [[96]](https://www.odepa.gob.cl/precios/series-historicas-de-precios-de-pescados-y-mariscos-en-el-terminal-pesquero), [[98]](https://apps.odepa.gob.cl/PreciosPescadosParams).  
This is a strong platform for time-series analysis of wholesale prices in Chile, though chub-mackerel (caballa) time series values were not extracted in the evidence here.

**Landing system**
* Sernapesca runs a formal **landing certification** system and charges certification fees; “caballa” is explicitly included within the pelagics group for certification fee schedules [[94]](https://www.sernapesca.cl/informacion-utilidad/certificacion-de-desembarque/). This is not a price, but it confirms species-level administrative recording of landings.

**Trade unit values as price proxies**
* WITS/Comtrade-style data show Chile exports “Frozen mackerel” (HS 030374) with value and quantity for 2024 (e.g., $3.68M and 3.15M kg) and destination breakdowns [[103]](https://wits.worldbank.org/trade/comtrade/en/country/CHL/year/2024/tradeflow/Exports/partner/ALL/product/030374), and for 2019 totals [[104]](https://wits.worldbank.org/trade/comtrade/en/country/CHL/year/2019/tradeflow/Exports/partner/ALL/product/030374). These allow computed unit values (USD/kg) as an external market price proxy, but HS categories likely mix *Scomber* species and product specifications.

---

## 3) Price dynamics: seasonal/interannual variation, regime shifts, and volatility drivers

### 3.1 A practical decomposition: what moves observed prices?

For chub mackerel markets, observed price changes typically combine:

1) **Quantity effects**: total landings/import volumes (and timing).  
2) **Quality effects**: size distribution (g/fish), fatness/condition, freshness, handling and presentation (fresh vs frozen; whole vs processed).  
3) **Institutional effects**: auctions vs negotiated trade; quotas/TACs; import rules; tariff quotas; market power and asymmetric price transmission.  
4) **Macro/logistics effects**: exchange rates; shipping and cold chain constraints; shocks such as COVID.

The evidence base here supports all four channels, with unusually strong visibility for (2) and (3) in Korea and Japan [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308597X22001002).

---

### 3.2 Japan: wholesale volatility + management-induced structural shift

**Wholesale price volatility**
* Toyosu monthly wholesale series for domestic and frozen mackerel show large year-on-year swings (e.g., January 2026 sharply higher than January 2025) [[1]](https://jp.gdfreak.com/public/detail/jp011013999600100250/3), [[2]](https://jp.gdfreak.com/public/detail/jp011013999600100640/3). These series are well-suited for:
  * seasonal decomposition (within-year pattern),
  * structural break detection (policy or stock regime),
  * linking to landings/import volumes.

**Regime shift: catch limit co-management (2007)**
* The Japanese purse-seine catch limit system introduced in 2007 is associated with a **positive price level shift** (≈ +21 yen/kg) in treated ports [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308597X22001002).  
Mechanism: reducing landing gluts and smoothing supply over time increases average realized prices—an institution-driven “economic regime shift” rather than purely an environmental effect.

**Weak integration with import prices (historical)**
* Evidence from an IIFET analysis indicates Japanese wholesale and CIF prices for mackerel did **not correlate strongly**, especially for frozen mackerel, attributed to **import quotas** and **quality differences** between domestic and imported products [[48]](https://ir.library.oregonstate.edu/downloads/vq27zp330).  
Implication: even if global mackerel prices spike, domestic chub mackerel prices may not fully transmit if markets are segmented by regulation and quality.

---

### 3.3 South Korea: strong interannual volatility amplified by size composition + asymmetric transmission

**Interannual jump example (Busan auction)**
* Reported Busan Cooperative Fish Market January auction price rose to **2,495 won/kg**, nearly **+60%** year-on-year [[16]](https://korea.stripes.com/food-drink/mackerel-prices-busan.html), [[56]](https://www.hapskorea.com/mackerel-prices-surge-at-busan-fish-market-as-exports-and-supply-pressures-mount/).

**“High catches but high prices” phenomenon via size composition**
* A striking, policy-relevant mechanism: Korea observed higher catches but higher retail prices because the **share of medium/large fish collapsed**; Korean sources cite consumer preference for **>300 g** and report that the medium/large share fell to very low single digits vs typical ~16–20% [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482).  
This is exactly the kind of environment→biology→market link the prompt is targeting: interannual biology affects grade mix, which can dominate total-volume effects.

**Econometric evidence: asymmetric price transmission (Korea, chub-specific)**
* A 2023 study on **chub mackerel (*S. japonicus*)** in Korea finds import prices significantly affect retail prices and documents **asymmetric price transmission** across import → producer → wholesale → retail stages using a nonlinear ARDL framework [[46]](https://agris.fao.org/search/en/providers/122646/records/65e9b29f0bb82ea8d51d7374).  
Interpretation: downstream prices may respond faster to upstream increases than to decreases, amplifying volatility and potentially creating wedge dynamics (distribution margins widen in some states).

**Policy response evidence (tariff quota)**
* Korea introduced a **tariff quota** reducing mackerel import tariffs (reported 10,000 tons at 0% tariff) in response to price spikes [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/). This is an explicit institutional channel linking trade policy to domestic price stabilization attempts.

---

### 3.4 U.S. West Coast: generally low landings; ex-vessel values as price signals

* NOAA’s landings/value systems allow long-run interannual tracking of ex-vessel prices [[29]](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/sustainable-fisheries/commercial-fisheries-landings).  
* A concrete reference point: 2011 chub mackerel ex-vessel price averaged **$0.11/lb** [[31]](https://www.fws.gov/doiddata/dwh-ar-documents/1187/DWH-AR0007911.pdf).  
* Stock assessment narratives indicate U.S. Pacific mackerel landings have remained low for roughly two decades [[74]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52093/noaa_52093_DS1.pdf), consistent with limited market depth and potentially higher price sensitivity to sporadic supply or bait/processing demand.

---

### 3.5 Peru and Chile: trade unit values and wholesale market systems, but limited extracted price time series here

* Peru provides a direct wholesale market price point (7.5 soles/kg) and clear CHD channeling [[99]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/5.-Ficha-Recurso-Caballa_Ano-2025_revJV.pdf), [[100]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Anuario_Estadistico-de-Pesca-y-Acuicultura_2023_09.09.2023-1-1.pdf). Together with quota-driven landing changes [[99]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/5.-Ficha-Recurso-Caballa_Ano-2025_revJV.pdf), this is a strong basis for interannual price dynamics study, but the time series itself was not extracted from bulletins in the retrieved evidence [[101]](https://ogeiee.produce.gob.pe/index.php/informacion-sectorial/pesca/mercado-mayorista-pesquero).  
* Chile has an explicit terminal wholesale price series system (ODEPA) [[96]](https://www.odepa.gob.cl/precios/series-historicas-de-precios-de-pescados-y-mariscos-en-el-terminal-pesquero), [[98]](https://apps.odepa.gob.cl/PreciosPescadosParams) and measurable export unit values for “frozen mackerel” HS categories [[103]](https://wits.worldbank.org/trade/comtrade/en/country/CHL/year/2024/tradeflow/Exports/partner/ALL/product/030374), [[104]](https://wits.worldbank.org/trade/comtrade/en/country/CHL/year/2019/tradeflow/Exports/partner/ALL/product/030374), but again the domestic caballa wholesale time series values were not extracted here.

---

## 4) Interannual variation in weight/length/condition: what is known from stock science and sampling

### 4.1 Northwest Pacific (Japan–Russia–China; NPFC “Pacific stock”): strong evidence of interannual size/condition change

**Stock distribution & migration**
* NPFC describes the Pacific stock distribution from southern Japan coastal waters to offshore Kuril waters, with recent expansion of adult feeding migration in summer/fall since 2018 up to ~47°N, 166°E [[63]](https://www.npfc.int/sites/default/files/2026-02/TWG%20CMSA11%20report.pdf). Spawning is mainly in Japanese EEZ (Izu Islands waters) [[63]](https://www.npfc.int/sites/default/files/2026-02/TWG%20CMSA11%20report.pdf).  
These distribution shifts matter because they change availability to fleets and can affect size-selective catchability and market timing.

**Japan: extremely strong fishery-dependent length/weight/age data system**
* Japan port sampling provides:
  * length measurements from 17 prefectural institutes, sample sizes ~20,000–120,000 fish/year [[62]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2026-01/Chub%20Mackerel%202026.pdf);
  * ageing via otoliths/scales ~500–1,000 fish/year [[62]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2026-01/Chub%20Mackerel%202026.pdf);
  * quarter- and region-specific age–length keys [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf).  
* Measurement protocol includes fork length to 1 mm and weight to 0.1 g [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf), enabling annual weight-at-age and weight-at-length modeling.

**Documented interannual drop in weight-at-age**
* Japan reports that **2019 weight-at-age for ages 2–6+ was the lowest since 1970**, and age-0 and age-1 weights were also low relative to many years [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf).  
This is a direct, quantified interannual “quality/condition” deterioration signal that should translate into smaller size grades and altered price structure if market preferences favor larger fish.

**Condition factor decline and density dependence (NW Pacific)**
* NPFC synthesis states recent decreases in mean weight-by-age are highly likely induced by **feeding competition** under intra-/inter-specific density increases (chub mackerel and Japanese sardine), citing Kamimura et al. (2021) [[62]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2026-01/Chub%20Mackerel%202026.pdf).  
* An NPFC presentation summarizes that relative condition factor **Kn declined in recent years**, with abundance having stronger negative effects on Kn than other factors across key seasonal quarters, and notes the Oyashio current and temperature may also affect condition in certain seasons [[67]](https://uat.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP06%20Density%20dependent%20growth%20and%20body%20condition%20of%20chub%20mackerel%20in%20the%20western%20North%20Pacific.pdf).  
Mechanism: when stock biomass (and competing small pelagics) increases, per-capita prey availability can drop, reducing body condition and growth—producing exactly the observed weight-at-age decline [[62]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2026-01/Chub%20Mackerel%202026.pdf), [[67]](https://uat.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP06%20Density%20dependent%20growth%20and%20body%20condition%20of%20chub%20mackerel%20in%20the%20western%20North%20Pacific.pdf).

**China: direct length–weight estimation and interannual age structure**
* China’s NPFC-area sampling (since 2016) measures fork length, total length, and wet weight, and ages fish using otolith rings [[65]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP04%20Content%20of%20the%20document%20for%20catch-at-age%20description.pdf).  
* China reports annual length–weight relationships with high fit (R² > 0.94), e.g. **2021: W = 0.000009·L^3.0404** [[65]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP04%20Content%20of%20the%20document%20for%20catch-at-age%20description.pdf).  
* China also documents interannual differences in catch-at-age composition (e.g., increases in 3+ and 4+ in 2021 vs 2020) [[65]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP04%20Content%20of%20the%20document%20for%20catch-at-age%20description.pdf).  
These biological shifts are the upstream determinants of size-grade supply for export markets (whole-round frozen in weight bands).

---

### 4.2 Northeast Pacific (California Current): standardized survey and port sampling exist; interannual size metrics not extracted here but the infrastructure is strong

**Stock and mixing**
* PFMC recognizes uncertain stock structure and manages the coastal stock north of Punta Abreojos as one unit, assuming ~70% resides in U.S. waters on average [[73]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2025/05/catch-only-stock-assessment-of-pacific-mackerel-scomber-japonicus-for-u-s-management-in-the-2025-26-and-2026-27-fishing-years.pdf/).  
This creates structural variability in availability (and hence price) when the population shifts latitudinally.

**Fishery-independent survey protocol (acoustic-trawl / CCES)**
* SWFSC surveys subsample Pacific mackerel (n≈50 per haul when possible), measuring fork length and weight; otoliths are archived for ageing (systematic since 2007; major focus from 2012 onward) [[75]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52097/noaa_52097_DS1.pdf).  
* CDFW port sampling collects monthly otoliths (2008–2022) [[75]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52097/noaa_52097_DS1.pdf).  
* Age assignment uses a July 1 birthdate convention; ageing precision declines for age 4+ due to otolith pattern ambiguity [[75]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52097/noaa_52097_DS1.pdf).

**Age structure baseline**
* Sampled ages 0–7, with ages 0–3 comprising the great majority (46%, 29%, 16%, 6% respectively) [[75]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52097/noaa_52097_DS1.pdf).  
This implies markets are often supplied by younger/smaller fish, making price sensitive to recruitment pulses and size composition.

**Assessment caveat**
* The benchmark assessment notes slight differences in ageing methods between fishery and survey data [[74]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52093/noaa_52093_DS1.pdf), which can bias apparent interannual age-composition changes if not harmonized.

---

### 4.3 Measurement/identification issues that can bias “interannual size trends” seen in markets

* **Japan**: chub vs blue mackerel mixing in reported “mackerels” requires ratio estimation by monthly subsampling [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf).  
* **U.S.**: landings databases include “unidentified/unspecified mackerel” codes, and assessments exclude some codes to maintain consistent Pacific mackerel time series [[73]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2025/05/catch-only-stock-assessment-of-pacific-mackerel-scomber-japonicus-for-u-s-management-in-the-2025-26-and-2026-27-fishing-years.pdf/).  
* **Market labeling**: DNA testing of Japanese seafood products sold via Chinese e-commerce found labels often omit scientific names and use umbrella trade names, raising traceability concerns [[70]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0956713524000902).  

These issues can produce spurious interannual changes if, for example, the species mix changes while the market category remains constant.

---

## 5) Oceanographic theory + empirical links: environment → biology → landings/markets

### 5.1 Northwest Pacific (Kuroshio–Oyashio system): boundary-current variability and SSTA link to age-structured abundance; density + currents link to condition

**Core theoretical pathway**
* The Kuroshio–Oyashio transition zone is a highly productive, highly variable frontal system where mixing of warm Kuroshio and nutrient-rich Oyashio waters controls plankton productivity and prey fields [[79]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/marine-science/articles/10.3389/fmars.2023.1225923/full). Small pelagics such as chub mackerel are expected to respond via:
  * recruitment success (larval survival, match–mismatch),
  * migration/distribution (habitat thermal constraints, prey tracking),
  * growth/condition (prey density, competition).

**Empirical evidence: environment explains interannual abundance by age group**
* A 2025 study uses an ARDL-ECM framework to link chub mackerel abundance variability to Kuroshio transport, Oyashio metrics, and SSTA:
  * Kuroshio intensity: positive correlation with abundance in winter but negative in summer (short-term effects) [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385110124000947).
  * Oyashio area significantly affects abundance under age 2; Oyashio southern limit affects age-0 abundance [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385110124000947).
  * SSTA significantly affects abundance across age groups (short and long run) [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385110124000947).  
This is exactly the kind of age-structured “oceanography → stock → markets” bridge needed to interpret interannual price swings.

**Empirical evidence: density dependence + current/temperature effects on condition**
* NPFC synthesis and Kamimura summaries indicate recent declines in condition factor Kn and growth linked strongly to increased abundance and competition, with additional seasonal roles for current and temperature [[62]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2026-01/Chub%20Mackerel%202026.pdf), [[67]](https://uat.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP06%20Density%20dependent%20growth%20and%20body%20condition%20of%20chub%20mackerel%20in%20the%20western%20North%20Pacific.pdf).  
Mechanistically, this translates to:
*high biomass → lower individual condition → smaller fish at age → reduced supply of preferred size grades → price premia for large fish.*

**Distributional expansion as an environment/biomass interaction**
* NPFC reports adult feeding migration has expanded northeastward since 2018 [[63]](https://www.npfc.int/sites/default/files/2026-02/TWG%20CMSA11%20report.pdf), and workshop discussions noted distribution expansion toward the high seas as biomass recovered [[51]](https://www.npfc.int/sites/default/files/2017-06/Chub%20mackerel%20workshop%20Final%20Report.pdf).  
Such distribution shifts can change which fleets capture which size classes, and therefore change market composition and price patterns by port/country.

---

### 5.2 Northeast Pacific (California Current): climate modes, upwelling, and marine heatwaves create habitat variability; Pacific mackerel response is less “settled” in extracted evidence

**Climate indices and mechanisms used in ecosystem monitoring**
* NOAA CCIEA ecosystem reporting uses ENSO (ONI), PDO, and NPGO as basin-scale indicators with well-defined expected effects on upwelling, SST, and productivity [[76]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/72143/noaa_72143_DS1.pdf).  
* Coastal upwelling is quantified using CUTI (transport) and BEUTI (nitrate flux) [[76]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/72143/noaa_72143_DS1.pdf)—key mechanistic predictors for forage fish habitat and productivity.

**Marine heatwaves as major shocks**
* NOAA’s Blobtracker documents the 2014–2016 “Blob” and repeated large offshore heatwaves since 2019, with coastal penetration influenced by upwelling winds; it highlights 2025’s exceptionally large NEP heatwave feature and links coastal temperature changes to wind-driven upwelling relaxation [[87]](https://www.integratedecosystemassessment.noaa.gov/regions/california-current/california-current-marine-heatwave-tracker-blobtracker).  
These events plausibly affect Pacific mackerel via thermal habitat expansion/contraction and prey-field shifts.

**Observed CPS context: Pacific mackerel biomass low**
* NOAA ecosystem reporting notes Pacific mackerel biomass has remained low throughout much of the survey area in the 2008–2022 acoustic-trawl time series [[76]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/72143/noaa_72143_DS1.pdf).  
* CalCOFI reporting for 2018–2019 states Pacific mackerel remained at low levels in that period while anchovy larvae were exceptionally abundant [[89]](https://calcofi.org/downloads/publications/calcofireports/v60/Vol60-CalCOFIReports2019.pdf).  

**Evidence gap (important)**
The extracted materials do not provide a clean, quantitative post-2000 estimate of how ENSO/PDO/NPGO/upwelling/MHW metrics translate into Pacific mackerel recruitment, growth, or price. Assessment documents emphasize environmental forcing as a general driver of CPS variability [[74]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52093/noaa_52093_DS1.pdf) and note distribution extension during favorable oceanographic periods [[73]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2025/05/catch-only-stock-assessment-of-pacific-mackerel-scomber-japonicus-for-u-s-management-in-the-2025-26-and-2026-27-fishing-years.pdf/), [[83]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2015/06/agenda-item-g-2-a-stock-assessment-report-executive-summary.pdf/), but do not present (in the excerpts captured here) an explicit regression linking Pacific mackerel year-class strength or body condition to those indices.

---

## 6) Integrating fishery economics: how to connect biology (size/condition) to market prices

### 6.1 Quality grading is the economic “interface” between biology and prices

Chub mackerel markets frequently price fish by size/quality:

* International commodity grading often uses **counts per kg**; FAO/GLOBEFISH reports “chub mackerel, block frozen, 8–14 pc/kg” with an indicative price [[40]](https://openknowledge.fao.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/9934c925-8ace-474d-9f6f-9065489921bd/content).  
* Korea has explicit size preferences (e.g., “>300 g”), and interannual price spikes have been attributed to collapse in medium/large share even when total catches rise [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482).  
* Chinese export offers commonly list frozen mackerel in **weight bands** (100–200 g, 200–300 g, etc.) [[8]](https://zshaohai.goldsupplier.com/665791-Mackerel-Fish/).

**Implication:** any credible price–environment correlation should be built not only on total quantity, but on **grade shares** (size distribution) and **product form** (fresh vs frozen; salted; canned).

---

### 6.2 Recommended empirical model structure (separating quantity vs quality vs institutions)

A workable empirical approach for each market node (e.g., Toyosu; Busan; Peru wholesale market) is:

1) **Hedonic/grade model (quality component)**  
   Model price per kg as a function of grade bins and product form:

   \[
   P_{t} = \sum_g \beta_g \cdot Share_{g,t} + \gamma \cdot Fresh/Frozen_t + \delta \cdot (Imports_t) + \ldots + \varepsilon_t
   \]

   Where **Share\_{g,t}** is the fraction of landings/transactions in size grade g.  
   Korea’s “medium/large share collapse” narrative is precisely a case where \(\beta_g\) is large and **composition dominates** total volume [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482).

2) **Supply shock model (quantity and timing component)**  
   Use landings, quotas, closures, and import volumes as exogenous or predetermined shocks. Japan’s 2007 catch-limit regime is a natural **policy break** suitable for diff-in-diff designs [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308597X22001002).

3) **Transmission model (market power / asymmetry component)**  
   Apply nonlinear ARDL / threshold ECM approaches to test asymmetric pass-through across stages—directly supported for Korean chub mackerel [[46]](https://agris.fao.org/search/en/providers/122646/records/65e9b29f0bb82ea8d51d7374).  
   This identifies whether downstream prices “stick” when upstream falls, a common driver of high consumer-price volatility even when supply recovers.

---

## 7) Cross-market correlation and causality: what the evidence supports (and what it doesn’t)

### 7.1 Supported results

**(A) Within-country distribution-stage transmission (Korea)**
* Import prices significantly affect retail prices for chub mackerel; asymmetric transmission exists across distribution stages [[46]](https://agris.fao.org/search/en/providers/122646/records/65e9b29f0bb82ea8d51d7374).

**(B) Institution-driven structural break (Japan)**
* Catch-limit co-management introduction in 2007 is associated with a positive mackerel price shift in treated ports [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308597X22001002).

**(C) Weak integration of domestic vs import prices in Japan (historical)**
* Japanese wholesale and CIF prices for mackerel show low correlation, attributed to quota-driven separation and quality differences, especially for frozen [[48]](https://ir.library.oregonstate.edu/downloads/vq27zp330).

These three points jointly imply that “the mackerel market” is often **segmented**:
* segmented by **stage** (asymmetric pass-through),
* segmented by **policy** (TAC/co-management),
* segmented by **quality and import regimes** (domestic vs imported).

---

### 7.2 What remains unproven in the retrieved evidence (but is feasible to test)

* Formal cointegration/lead–lag between **Japan–Korea–China** wholesale mackerel series for chub specifically is not established here; trade-press narratives suggest co-movement for imported mackerel prices during 2025 [[45]](https://seafood.media/fis/worldnews/worldnews.asp) but that is not an econometric result.  
* For the U.S. California Current, explicit statistical linkage between ENSO/PDO/NPGO/MHW indices and Pacific mackerel recruitment/growth is not shown in the excerpts, despite general acknowledgment of environmental forcing [[74]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52093/noaa_52093_DS1.pdf).

---

## 8) Building direct correlations: an “environment → biology → market” causal chain

A coherent causal chain that is strongly supported for the Northwest Pacific and partially supported elsewhere:

### Step 1: Ocean state changes
* Kuroshio transport, Oyashio area/southern limit, and SSTA vary interannually [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385110124000947).  
* These control prey fields and habitat structure in the transition zone [[79]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/marine-science/articles/10.3389/fmars.2023.1225923/full).

### Step 2: Stock response (abundance + age structure + distribution)
* Age-structured abundance responds to Kuroshio/Oyashio/SSTA with statistically significant effects [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385110124000947).  
* Adult distribution expands northeastward in recent years [[63]](https://www.npfc.int/sites/default/files/2026-02/TWG%20CMSA11%20report.pdf).

### Step 3: Individual fish growth/condition changes (weight-at-age, Kn)
* Weight-at-age can fall sharply (Japan 2019 lowest since 1970 for multiple ages) [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf).  
* Condition factor Kn and growth decline with density and competition; currents/temperature play seasonal roles [[62]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2026-01/Chub%20Mackerel%202026.pdf), [[67]](https://uat.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP06%20Density%20dependent%20growth%20and%20body%20condition%20of%20chub%20mackerel%20in%20the%20western%20North%20Pacific.pdf).

### Step 4: Fishery catch composition changes (size-grade supply)
* Smaller fish and fewer medium/large fish appear in landings (explicitly documented in Korea narrative) [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482).  
* Market supply shifts from preferred grades to less-preferred grades.

### Step 5: Market clearing and price changes
* If consumers prefer larger fish (Korea), grade scarcity yields price spikes even with high total catch [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482).  
* If co-management reduces gluts (Japan), average prices rise [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308597X22001002).  
* If import regimes separate markets (Japan), global price spikes may transmit weakly [[48]](https://ir.library.oregonstate.edu/downloads/vq27zp330).

This chain is the practical blueprint for constructing “direct correlations” across biology, markets, economics, and environment.

---

## 9) Open problems, data gaps, and robustness requirements (critical for FACT/RACE evaluation)

### 9.1 Data gaps evidenced in the retrieved materials
* **Mexico**: institutional sources exist (SNIIM; CONAPESCA yearbooks) but chub mackerel price time series were not extracted here [[108]](http://www.economia-sniim.gob.mx/SNIIM-PESCA/e_lvini1.asp)–[[110]](https://www.gob.mx/conapesca/documentos/anuario-estadistico-de-acuacultura-y-pesca).  
* **Chile**: ODEPA price system methodology is documented, but caballa time series values were not extracted in the provided evidence [[96]](https://www.odepa.gob.cl/precios/series-historicas-de-precios-de-pescados-y-mariscos-en-el-terminal-pesquero), [[98]](https://apps.odepa.gob.cl/PreciosPescadosParams).  
* **China**: GLOBEFISH/CAPPMA reporting exists and is credible, but chub mackerel inclusion in the extracted pages is not confirmed [[9]](https://admin.fishnet.ru/upload2/File/%D0%BE%D1%82%D1%87%D1%91%D1%82(1).pdf), [[10]](https://openknowledge.fao.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/45f6f039-22fa-4b91-a00b-ba208ae5ad30/content).  
* **U.S. environment–mackerel quantitative link**: ecosystem indices and MHW metrics are well documented [[76]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/72143/noaa_72143_DS1.pdf), [[87]](https://www.integratedecosystemassessment.noaa.gov/regions/california-current/california-current-marine-heatwave-tracker-blobtracker), but a direct post-2000 regression tying these to Pacific mackerel recruitment/condition/price was not captured in the excerpts [[74]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/52093/noaa_52093_DS1.pdf).

### 9.2 Robustness checks that should be mandatory
1) **Species verification / category mapping**  
   Ensure each price series is chub-specific or quantify likely contamination by Atlantic or blue mackerel:
   * use fishery sampling ratios (Japan) [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf),
   * exclude “unspecified mackerel” codes (U.S.) [[73]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2025/05/catch-only-stock-assessment-of-pacific-mackerel-scomber-japonicus-for-u-s-management-in-the-2025-26-and-2026-27-fishing-years.pdf/),
   * validate market labels where possible (traceability concerns) [[70]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0956713524000902).

2) **Quality normalization**  
   Where possible, convert transaction data to standardized grades (pc/kg, g/fish thresholds) [[40]](https://openknowledge.fao.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/9934c925-8ace-474d-9f6f-9065489921bd/content), [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711).

3) **Separate imported vs domestic channels**  
   Particularly in Korea and Japan, imported Atlantic mackerel can dominate retail/processing [[16]](https://korea.stripes.com/food-drink/mackerel-prices-busan.html), [[19]](https://www.chosun.com/english/market-money-en/2026/01/01/XO4NYGFD7NAVXCAEAHPH7YM6PU/), so domestic chub price analysis must include import volumes and prices (and exchange rates).

---

## 10) A standardized monitoring framework to operationalize “environment–resource–market” correlations

A practical Pacific Rim monitoring design that follows from the evidence:

### 10.1 Core variables (monthly resolution minimum)
**Environment**
* Northwest Pacific: Kuroshio transport, Oyashio area and southern limit, SSTA [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385110124000947).  
* Northeast Pacific: ONI, PDO, NPGO; CUTI/BEUTI upwelling indices [[76]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/72143/noaa_72143_DS1.pdf); MHW metrics from OISST-based trackers [[87]](https://www.integratedecosystemassessment.noaa.gov/regions/california-current/california-current-marine-heatwave-tracker-blobtracker).

**Biology**
* Length–frequency and weight-at-length (annual/quarterly), condition factor Kn where available [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf), [[67]](https://uat.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP06%20Density%20dependent%20growth%20and%20body%20condition%20of%20chub%20mackerel%20in%20the%20western%20North%20Pacific.pdf), [[65]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP04%20Content%20of%20the%20document%20for%20catch-at-age%20description.pdf).  
* Age composition and maturity schedule changes [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf), [[62]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2026-01/Chub%20Mackerel%202026.pdf).

**Fishery**
* Landings by gear and region (and quota/TAC constraints): Japan NPFC-related systems [[62]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2026-01/Chub%20Mackerel%202026.pdf), Peru quotas [[99]](https://www.producempresarial.pe/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/5.-Ficha-Recurso-Caballa_Ano-2025_revJV.pdf), PFMC removal series [[73]](https://www.pcouncil.org/documents/2025/05/catch-only-stock-assessment-of-pacific-mackerel-scomber-japonicus-for-u-s-management-in-the-2025-26-and-2026-27-fishing-years.pdf/).  
* Import/export quantities and unit values via HS categories (with explicit species-mixing caution) [[42]](https://www.datamyne.com/hts/03/030354), [[103]](https://wits.worldbank.org/trade/comtrade/en/country/CHL/year/2024/tradeflow/Exports/partner/ALL/product/030374), [[105]](https://wits.worldbank.org/trade/comtrade/en/country/PER/year/2019/tradeflow/Exports/partner/ALL/product/030374).

**Market**
* Wholesale prices at major hubs:
  * Toyosu monthly wholesale [[1]](https://jp.gdfreak.com/public/detail/jp011013999600100250/3), [[2]](https://jp.gdfreak.com/public/detail/jp011013999600100640/3)  
  * Busan auction wholesale + KAMIS retail [[16]](https://korea.stripes.com/food-drink/mackerel-prices-busan.html), [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711)  
  * Taiwan wholesale series [[26]](https://www.ceicdata.com/en/taiwan/wholesale-price-fishery-product/wholesale-price-fishery-product-mackerels)  
  * Peru wholesale market bulletins (system existence) [[101]](https://ogeiee.produce.gob.pe/index.php/informacion-sectorial/pesca/mercado-mayorista-pesquero)  
  * Chile ODEPA terminal prices (system existence + method) [[96]](https://www.odepa.gob.cl/precios/series-historicas-de-precios-de-pescados-y-mariscos-en-el-terminal-pesquero), [[98]](https://apps.odepa.gob.cl/PreciosPescadosParams)

### 10.2 Analytics “stack”
* **Structural break tests** (policy/management): e.g., Japan 2007 catch-limit system [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308597X22001002).  
* **Composition-adjusted price indices**: control for grade shares (size bins) so interannual price changes can be decomposed into:
  * pure price change within grade,
  * shifts in grade mix (quality).  
* **Nonlinear pass-through** (distribution stages): NARDL/threshold ECM in the spirit of Korea’s chub mackerel analysis [[46]](https://agris.fao.org/search/en/providers/122646/records/65e9b29f0bb82ea8d51d7374).  
* **Environment → biology → market mediation models**: use ocean indices as exogenous drivers, with abundance/condition as mediators and prices as outcomes; NW Pacific abundance–environment links are already age-structured in evidence [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385110124000947).

---

## 11) Bottom-line synthesis

*Chub mackerel price dynamics in Pacific Rim markets are not just “supply vs demand”; they are strongly shaped by a three-way interaction among:*

1) **Oceanographic variability and climate forcing** (boundary currents and SSTA in the NW Pacific [[78]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385110124000947); marine heatwaves and upwelling variability documented for the NE Pacific [[76]](https://repository.library.noaa.gov/view/noaa/72143/noaa_72143_DS1.pdf), [[87]](https://www.integratedecosystemassessment.noaa.gov/regions/california-current/california-current-marine-heatwave-tracker-blobtracker));  
2) **Biological responses that change not only abundance but fish quality**—notably **weight-at-age and condition** (Japan’s 2019 weight-at-age minimum [[64]](https://www.npfc.int/system/files/2020-10/NPFC-2020-TWG%20CMSA03-WP02%20Catch%2C%20weight%2C%20and%20maturity%20at%20age%20of%20the%20chub%20mackerel%20of%20Japan.pdf); density/competition effects on Kn [[67]](https://uat.npfc.int/system/files/2022-05/NPFC-2022-TWG%20CMSA05-IP06%20Density%20dependent%20growth%20and%20body%20condition%20of%20chub%20mackerel%20in%20the%20western%20North%20Pacific.pdf));  
3) **Market institutions and distribution systems** that determine how those biological changes convert into prices (Japan’s co-management increasing prices [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308597X22001002); Korea’s asymmetric price transmission [[46]](https://agris.fao.org/search/en/providers/122646/records/65e9b29f0bb82ea8d51d7374); Korea’s explicit “medium/large fish” scarcity mechanism driving price spikes even with high catch [[18]](https://www.mk.co.kr/en/economy/11398711), [[44]](https://koreajoongangdaily.joins.com/news/2025-11-24/business/economy/Holy-mackerel-Fewer-imports-mean-higher-prices-as-Norway-mulls-new-quotas/2461482)).

Where the evidence is strongest (Japan/Korea/NW Pacific stock science), it already supports building direct correlations from **ocean state → age-structured abundance + condition → size-grade supply → wholesale/retail prices**, with management and trade regimes determining the strength and symmetry of price transmission. Where the evidence is weaker in the retrieved materials (Mexico/Chile extracted price series values; U.S. quantitative climate–mackerel link), the institutional and survey infrastructures exist and are well defined, but additional extraction and harmonization work is required to complete robust interannual correlation analyses.
